name: Kitsune Circuit
description: A winding eastward path known to Kitsune wanderers, marked by carved wooden posts and small offerings left at regular intervals. The corruption pressure here is notably lower — whether by design or by the Kitsune's particular relationship with it is unclear.
ambient_lines: A carved post with a kitsune face at the path's edge. Fresh oil on the wood — maintained recently.
The path turns in ways that seem arbitrary until you realize they avoid specific clearings.
Small cloth pouches hang from branches. You don't open them. You feel they're not for you.
A Kitsune camp, cold but recently vacated. The fire ring is swept, the space left clean.
The corruption feels attenuated here — present but held at distance, like sound through glass.
Movement in the underbrush — deliberate, paced. Something walking parallel to the path.
A wind chime of bones and river glass hung between two trees. It sings in no wind.
The path markings shift subtly. You wonder if the route itself is adjustable.
Footprints ahead — bare, clawed, spaced for something that moves very fast.
A smell of fox and cedar and something floral that has no name in any language you know.
One of the carved posts has been damaged — a clean strike, like a blade. Recently.
The light in this stretch is unusually warm. No source accounts for it.
You find a small clay figure of a Kitsune on a rock beside the path. It's looking backward.
The circuit bends around a large hollow tree. Inside is dry, swept, and empty. Waiting.
Bells in the middle distance. A pattern. Not random. Communication.
The corruption's absence here has a quality — like pressure released. Your head feels clearer.
Two paths diverge. Both are marked. The right-hand post has a fresh notch that wasn't cut yesterday.
A Kitsune sits cross-legged on a rock, watching you. When you look directly, they are gone.
The circuit narrows to single-file through dense undergrowth. On the other side, a meadow.
Something left a meal on a stone — fruit, dried meat, a waterskin. Placed for someone, recently.

time_variants.dawn: The Kitsune are most active at dawn. The circuit feels populated even when you can't see anyone.
time_variants.morning: Morning on the circuit is genuinely peaceful. The absence of corruption pressure is noticeable.
time_variants.midday: At midday the wanderers rest. The path is empty and the silence is voluntary, not threatening.
time_variants.evening: Evening brings a particular quality of gold light through the circuit's canopy. Something about it feels like a gift.
time_variants.night: At night, the circuit's carved posts glow faintly at their bases. Bioluminescence. Probably bioluminescence.

points_of_interest.name: The Fox Post
points_of_interest.desc: The oldest carved marker on the circuit — a six-tailed kitsune, the carving deep and worn. Offerings are placed here continuously. You have never seen the person who places them.

points_of_interest.name: The Meeting Hollow
points_of_interest.desc: A large clearing at the circuit's midpoint with a central fire pit and seating for twenty. Used for wanderer gatherings. The next one is in two weeks, if the calendar on the post is accurate.

points_of_interest.name: The Corruption Seal
points_of_interest.desc: An area where the ground has been worked with Kitsune ritual — carved channels, salt lines, something buried. The corruption near it reads as actively suppressed, not absent.

arrival_scene: The circuit announces itself with a carved post at the tree line — a kitsune face worn smooth by weather and touch, maintained with oil. The corruption pressure, which you have come to experience as a kind of low tonal hum at the back of your skull, drops noticeably within twenty steps of the post. You don't know whether to be grateful or suspicious. Probably both.

connections.name: Wolf-Road Edge
connections.desc: West, back to the main road. The corruption pressure rises within a kilometer of the junction.

connections.name: Cult Ascent
connections.desc: North, where the circuit ends and the cult's territory begins. The carved posts stop at the boundary. Deliberately.
